strategic_ideas: Control the center with pawns and develop pieces toward it.
Castle early to protect the king. Avoid moving the same piece twice without good reason.
Your repertoire as White: 1.d4 leading to London System or Queen's Gambit.

d4.label: Queen's Pawn (Your Move)
d4.prose: Your main weapon as White. Controls e5 and c5, leads to the London System (2.Nf3 + 3.Bf4) or Queen's Gambit (2.c4). Solid, positional, and favored by world champions.
d4.follow_up_idea: After 1.d4 d5 2.Nf3 you're heading toward the London. After 2.c4 it's the Queen's Gambit. Both are excellent for practical play.

e4.label: King's Pawn (Not Your Line)
e4.prose: The most popular first move — open, tactical, and forcing. You prefer 1.d4, but understanding e4 openings helps you as Black when facing them.
e4.follow_up_idea: You'll face 1.e4 as Black. Your responses: 1...c6 (Caro-Kann) or 1...e6 (French Defense).

Nf3.label: Réti / Flexible
Nf3.prose: Delays committing a center pawn. Can transpose to London (d4+Bf4) or Queen's Gambit. Useful when you want to see Black's setup first.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 1.Nf3 d5 2.d4 you've reached the London starting position by transposition.

c4.label: English Opening
c4.prose: Flank control of d5. Often transposes to Queen's Gambit after 1.c4 e5 2.Nc3 or 1.c4 Nf6 2.Nf3 d5 3.d4. Less forcing than 1.d4.
c4.follow_up_idea: The English is solid but you'll get more familiar positions starting with 1.d4 directly.

strategic_ideas: You've played 1.d4 — now steer toward the London System (2.Nf3 + 3.Bf4) or Queen's Gambit (2.c4).
London System is simpler: same setup against almost anything. QGD is more ambitious and theory-heavy.
Watch Black's response: 1...d5 and 1...Nf6 are the main moves.

d5.label: Symmetric Center
d5.prose: The most natural reply — Black mirrors your center claim. Now you can go London (2.Nf3) or Queen's Gambit (2.c4). Both are in your repertoire.
d5.follow_up_idea: 2.Nf3 heads toward London; 2.c4 starts the Queen's Gambit. London is simpler and recommended when you want a solid, familiar structure.

Nf6.label: Indian Setup
Nf6.prose: Black delays ...d5 and waits. After 2.Nf3 d5 3.Bf4 you're in London territory. If Black fianchettoes with ...g6 it may become King's Indian — you can still play London setup.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: 2.Nf3 d5 3.Bf4 keeps you in London System. Against 2...g6 play 3.Bf4 anyway and build e3+Bd3 setup.

e6.label: Dutch/French Setup
e6.prose: Flexible — could be French Defense setup or Dutch (if ...f5 follows). After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 you're in London territory.
e6.follow_up_idea: Don't panic about the French setup vs d4 — just play 2.Nf3 and develop normally. Black's ...e6 is solid but passive.

f5.label: Dutch Defense
f5.prose: Aggressive — Black grabs kingside space immediately. Risky but tricky. After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 or 2.Bg5 you can take Black out of their prep.
f5.follow_up_idea: 2.Bg5 is an annoying anti-Dutch weapon. Or just play 2.Nf3 and London setup — the Dutch bishop on f8 will be passive behind the f-pawn.

c5.label: Benoni Setup
c5.prose: Black immediately challenges with the flank pawn. After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.d5 it becomes a Benoni. You can also play 2.Nf3 + 3.Bf4 for London-style development.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 1.d4 c5 2.Nf3 cxd4 3.Nxd4 you're in Sicilian-like territory. Alternatively stay flexible with 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4.

strategic_ideas: The symmetrical pawn duo. You must choose: London System (2.Nf3 → 3.Bf4) or Queen's Gambit (2.c4).
London is your safer, more practical choice at 700 ELO — same setup game after game, less theory.
Queen's Gambit is more ambitious: 2.c4 attacks d5 immediately and creates concrete tactical play.

Nf3.label: London System (Recommended)
Nf3.prose: The London move order. After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 you've set up the London System — a solid, piece-first opening with minimal pawn weaknesses. Your bishop gets to f4 before Black can challenge it.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 you exchange bishops on Black's terms, maintaining a safe structure. Or 3.Bf4 c5 4.e3 and carry on.

c4.label: Queen's Gambit
c4.prose: Attacks the d5 pawn immediately — the Queen's Gambit. More forcing than the London. Black can accept (2...dxc4) or decline (2...e6 — QGD). You get an active bishop and central control.
c4.follow_up_idea: After 2.c4 e6 3.Nc3 Nf6 4.Bg5 you're in the Classical QGD — solid and well-tested. After 2.c4 dxc4 (QGA) play 3.Nf3 and recoup the pawn with e3.

Bf4.label: Direct London (2.Bf4)
Bf4.prose: Develop the bishop before playing Nf3. Slightly less flexible but gets the key piece out early. Works fine — just means 2...c5 by Black can be tricky if you're not careful.
Bf4.follow_up_idea: After 2.Bf4 Nf6 3.Nf3 you're in standard London. Against 2.Bf4 c5 play 3.e3 and don't rush d5.

strategic_ideas: You've started the London. The knight develops naturally and prepares Bf4.
Black's most natural reply is 2...Nf6, developing symmetrically. Now play 3.Bf4 to claim the bishop's ideal square.
The London bishop on f4 is the cornerstone of your setup — get it there before Black can play ...Bf5.

Nf6.label: Main Line
Nf6.prose: The most natural developing move for Black. Now play 3.Bf4 immediately — the London bishop must reach f4 before Black plays ...Bf5 (stealing your square).
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 2...Nf6 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 — Black challenges your bishop but you exchange it favorably, keeping a solid structure.

c5.label: Aggressive Counter
c5.prose: Black immediately challenges the center. Don't panic — just continue with 3.Bf4 or 3.e3 and maintain your London setup. Avoid dxc5 unless you have a clear plan.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 2...c5 3.Bf4 Nf6 4.e3 cxd4 5.exd4 you have a slightly open London where your bishop pair is active.

e6.label: French-Like Setup
e6.prose: Solid but passive. Play 3.Bf4 and build the London. Black will likely follow with ...Nf6 and ...Bd6.
e6.follow_up_idea: After 2...e6 3.Bf4 Nf6 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 — identical to the main line. The London reaches the same structure by multiple routes.

strategic_ideas: Both knights are developed. Now play 3.Bf4 — the defining move of the London System.
The bishop on f4 is outside your pawn chain. It develops before e3 so it's not locked in.
This is the critical London move order. Don't play 3.e3 before Bf4 — that locks the bishop in.

Bf4.label: The London Move — Key!
Bf4.prose: Essential. The c1 bishop gets to its ideal square BEFORE you play e3. After 3.Bf4 Black must decide: accept the structure (3...e6) or challenge immediately (3...c5). Either way you have a solid setup.
Bf4.follow_up_idea: After 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 — now e3 is fine because the bishop is already out. Continue with Bd3, Nbd2, c3, and castle short.

strategic_ideas: The London System is set. Your bishop is active on f4, targeting c7 and the queenside.
Now let Black commit. Main responses: 3...e6 (solid, leads to Bd6 plan), 3...c5 (challenges center), 3...Bf5 (tries to mirror your bishop).
Your plan: e3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3, castle kingside. Straightforward and very solid.

e6.label: Main Line — Solid
e6.prose: Black prepares ...Bd6 to challenge your bishop. After 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 Black exchanges your bishop (or retreats). Either way you maintain a safe, solid position. White has slightly easier development.
e6.follow_up_idea: After 3...e6 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 Bxg3 6.hxg3 — you open the h-file, which can be useful later. Continue Bd3, Nbd2, c3, 0-0.

c5.label: Aggressive Counter
c5.prose: Black fights for the center. Play 4.e3 maintaining the structure. After 4...Nc6 5.c3 e6 you're in a solid London with extra central tension. Don't take on c5 unless you get a clear advantage.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 3...c5 4.e3 Nc6 5.c3 e6 6.Bd3 Bd6 7.Bxd6 Qxd6 8.Nbd2 0-0 9.0-0 you have a pleasant, solid London position.

Bf5.label: Mirroring Your Bishop
Bf5.prose: Black copies your bishop development. Play 4.e3 (not Nh4 yet — develop first). After ...e6, Bd3 and you can later play Nh4 to swap bishops if desired.
Bf5.follow_up_idea: After 3...Bf5 4.e3 e6 5.Bd3 Bxd3 6.Qxd3 Bd6 — the bishops are exchanged and you have a symmetrical position with slightly easier play.

strategic_ideas: Black has played the solid ...e6. Now 4.e3 completes the London core setup.
After e3 your plan is: Bd3, Nbd2, c3, castle kingside. Simple and effective.
Black will likely play ...Bd6 next, challenging your f4 bishop — the critical moment.

e3.label: Complete the London Setup
e3.prose: Essential — supports d4, opens the diagonal for your f1-bishop, and prepares Bd3. The London pawn structure (d4-e3-c3) is extremely solid. Black has no easy way to create imbalance.
e3.follow_up_idea: After 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 — when Black tries to trade your f4-bishop, you retreat to g3 rather than exchange. This keeps your solid structure intact.

strategic_ideas: Black plays ...Bd6 to attack your f4 bishop. This is the most important decision point in the London.
Do NOT take on d6 (it opens the file for Black's queen). Instead play 5.Bg3 — retreat the bishop.
After Bg3 Black must either exchange on g3 (giving you the half-open h-file) or retreat their bishop.

Bg3.label: The Correct Retreat
Bg3.prose: Preserve the bishop! After 5.Bg3 Bxg3 6.hxg3 the h-file is half-open — not a weakness, actually a long-term asset. If Black retreats with 5...Be7 instead, you just continue with Bd3, Nbd2, 0-0.
Bg3.follow_up_idea: After 5.Bg3 Bxg3 6.hxg3 0-0 7.Bd3 c5 8.c3 Nc6 9.Nbd2 e5 10.dxe5 Nxe5 — White has solid structure and the half-open h-file for kingside play.

strategic_ideas: Your bishop is safely on g3. Black must now decide whether to exchange on g3 or keep pieces.
If Black takes on g3: you recapture hxg3 and gain the h-file — a long-term kingside asset.
Continue: Bd3, Nbd2, c3, castle kingside. Then plan h4-h5 or Qc2 + Nh4 depending on the position.

Bxg3.label: Exchange — You Get h-file
Bxg3.prose: Black trades the bishop. After 6.hxg3 you have a half-open h-file and a doubled g-pawn that isn't a weakness — it actually controls f4 and h4. Castle and prepare h4 for kingside pressure.
Bxg3.follow_up_idea: After 5...Bxg3 6.hxg3 O-O 7.Bd3 c5 8.c3 Nc6 9.Nbd2 — solid London. Long-term White can play Qc2, g4, Rh1 for kingside attack.

Be7.label: Retreat — Keep Pieces
Be7.prose: Black retreats and prepares to castle. Continue with 6.Bd3 Nbd7 7.Nbd2 0-0 8.0-0 — your setup is complete and you have a comfortable, solid position.
Be7.follow_up_idea: After 5...Be7 6.Bd3 0-0 7.0-0 c5 8.c3 Nc6 9.Nbd2 — standard London. You can consider Ne5 later or prepare a queenside expansion with b3/b4.

strategic_ideas: Black plays ...c5 challenging d4. Don't panic — just play 4.e3 and maintain your structure.
You can let Black take on d4 (dxc5 or cxd4) and recapture with pieces — you keep your London development.
Avoid dxc5 yourself unless you have a specific tactical reason — it frees Black's game.

e3.label: Maintain Structure
e3.prose: Continue the London setup. After 4.e3 Nc6 5.c3 e6 6.Bd3 Bd6 7.Bxd6 Qxd6 8.Nbd2 you have a solid position. Black's ...c5 hasn't created any problems.
e3.follow_up_idea: After 3...c5 4.e3 cxd4 5.exd4 (or Nxd4) you reach an isolated queen pawn position where your pieces are active. This is fine for White.

strategic_ideas: Black plays 1...Nf6 (Indian Defense setup). After 2.Nf3 Black will decide between ...d5 (London territory) or ...g6 (KID/Grünfeld).
If 2...d5 you get a normal London. If 2...g6 or 2...e6, just develop with 3.Bf4 and maintain your setup.
The London works against almost all Indian setups — just keep playing Bf4, e3, Bd3, c3.

d5.label: Transposes to London
d5.prose: Now 3.Bf4 and you're in a normal London System by transposition. Identical to the 1.d4 d5 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 line.
d5.follow_up_idea: 3.Bf4 then e3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3, 0-0 — standard London plan.

g6.label: KID Setup — Stay London
g6.prose: Black aims for King's Indian Defense. Play 3.Bf4 anyway. After ...Bg7 4.e3 0-0 5.Bd3 d6 6.0-0 — you're in an Anti-KID London. Your solid setup makes the KID fianchetto less scary.
g6.follow_up_idea: In London vs KID: keep your center solid (d4, e3, c3) and don't get drawn into pawn storms. Black needs ...e5 to free their game — your d4+e3 structure resists this.

e6.label: Queen's Indian Setup
e6.prose: 3.Bf4 and develop normally. Black is playing a passive setup — you'll have the easier development.
e6.follow_up_idea: After 2...e6 3.Bf4 b6 (Queen's Indian) 4.e3 Bb7 5.Bd3 — solid London. Black's queenside fianchetto isn't dangerous.

strategic_ideas: The London is established via 1.d4 Nf6 2.Nf3 d5 3.Bf4 — identical position to the main line.
Continue with 4.e3 after Black's response. The same London plans apply regardless of move order.
Black often plays ...e6, ...c5, or ...c6 here — all handled the same way.

e6.label: Standard London Structure
e6.prose: Same as the main London line. Play 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 and follow the standard plan: Bd3, Nbd2, c3, 0-0.
e6.follow_up_idea: After 3...e6 4.e3 Bd6 5.Bg3 — the classic London structure develops naturally.

c5.label: Active Counterplay
c5.prose: 4.e3 and maintain structure. Don't swap the d-pawn for the c-pawn unless forced.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 3...c5 4.e3 cxd4 5.exd4 you have an isolated queen pawn but very active pieces.

Bf5.label: Mirror Bishop
Bf5.prose: Black mirrors your f4-bishop with ...Bf5. Play 4.e3 e6 5.Bd3 Bxd3 6.Qxd3 — the exchange is fine for you.
Bf5.follow_up_idea: After 3...Bf5 4.e3 e6 5.Bd3 Bxd3 6.Qxd3 c5 7.c3 Nc6 8.Nbd2 — solid equality with easier development for White.

strategic_ideas: Black declines the gambit with the solid ...e6. This is the QGD — one of Black's best responses.
White has many systems: 3.Nc3 (Classical), 3.Nf3 (flexible), 3.cxd5 (Exchange QGD).
Your main plan: develop with Nc3, Nf3, and Bg5 (the Classical QGD) or keep it simpler with Nf3+Bf4.

Nc3.label: Classical — 3.Nc3
Nc3.prose: The main line. Develops the knight and prepares Bg5 (pinning Black's knight and increasing central pressure). After 3...Nf6 4.Bg5 you're in Classical QGD territory.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nc3 Nf6 4.Bg5 Be7 5.e3 0-0 6.Nf3 — you have a solid, classical setup. Black's position is solid but slightly passive.

Nf3.label: Flexible — 3.Nf3
Nf3.prose: Develop the knight first and decide Bg5 or Bf4 later. After 3...Nf6 4.Nc3 you reach the same Classical positions. Or try 4.Bg5 directly.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.Bg5 Be7 5.Nc3 — transposing to Classical QGD. The move order doesn't matter much at this level.

cxd5.label: Exchange QGD
cxd5.prose: Simplifies with a symmetrical pawn structure. White gets a slight space advantage and the c-file after Nc3, Bf4, and sometimes c4 re-expansion. Less theory but less advantage too.
cxd5.follow_up_idea: After 3.cxd5 exd5 4.Nc3 Nf6 5.Bf4 — White targets the queenside with minority attack (b4-b5). Simple but effective at club level.

strategic_ideas: The Classical QGD tabiya. Now 4.Bg5 is the signature move — pinning Black's knight.
Black cannot comfortably play ...dxc4 while the knight is pinned (Bxf6 followed by Qd8+ tricks).
Your target: keep the pin, play e3, Nf3, and build central pressure.

Bg5.label: Classical Pin — Recommended
Bg5.prose: The most ambitious move. The pin on Nf6 is annoying for Black and creates long-term pressure. Black must decide: ...Be7 (solid), ...h6 (asking the question), or ...Bb4 (Ragozin).
Bg5.follow_up_idea: After 4.Bg5 Be7 5.e3 0-0 6.Nf3 Nbd7 7.Rc1 c6 8.Bd3 — you're in the main QGD. White is solid with long-term queenside pressure.

Nf3.label: Flexible Development
Nf3.prose: Develop naturally and keep options open. After 4.Nf3 Be7 5.Bg5 — you end up in the same position as 4.Bg5 anyway.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: Either move order works — the Classical QGD structure is very solid regardless.

strategic_ideas: Black develops solidly with ...Be7. Continue with 5.e3 completing your development.
After 5.e3 0-0 6.Nf3 you have a flexible, solid setup. Black's position is slightly cramped.
Long-term plan: minority attack on queenside (b4-b5) or central pressure with e4.

e3.label: Complete the Setup
e3.prose: 5.e3 is the only good move — completes development and prepares Nf3 and Bd3. After 5.e3 0-0 6.Nf3 Nbd7 7.Rc1 c6 8.Bd3 you're in the main QGD tabiya.
e3.follow_up_idea: After 5.e3 0-0 6.Nf3 h6 7.Bh4 (maintain the pin) or 7.Bxf6 (exchange, simplified) — both are fine. The classical plan is Bd3, 0-0, then b4-b5 minority attack.

strategic_ideas: Black accepts the gambit! The pawn on c4 is won temporarily — White will recoup it.
Play 3.Nf3 — develop and prepare to win back c4 with e3+Bxc4 or e4.
Don't rush to take back immediately with Qa4+ or e3 — develop first.

Nf3.label: Recommended — Develop First
Nf3.prose: The most natural response. After 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.e3 (or 4.Nc3) you prepare to recapture c4 with Bxc4. Black cannot keep the pawn.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.e3 e6 5.Bxc4 c5 6.0-0 — you've won back the pawn and have an active bishop on c4. The position is open and dynamic.

e3.label: Direct Recapture Setup
e3.prose: Preparing Bxc4. After 3.e3 Nf6 4.Bxc4 e6 5.Nf3 — the same structure as after 3.Nf3 just in different order.
e3.follow_up_idea: Fine, but 3.Nf3 first is slightly more flexible.

strategic_ideas: Both knights are developed. Now 4.e3 prepares to recapture the c4 pawn with Bxc4.
After Bxc4 your bishop is very active on the a2-g8 diagonal, targeting f7.
The QGA leads to open, active positions — White has easy development.

e3.label: Recapture the Pawn
e3.prose: After 4.e3 e6 5.Bxc4 c5 6.0-0 — the pawn is recaptured and your c4 bishop is excellent. Black's structure is solid but White has more active pieces.
e3.follow_up_idea: After 5.Bxc4 Black typically plays 5...c5 (challenging d4) or 5...a6 (preparing ...b5). Both lead to open, dynamic positions where your piece activity compensates for the open position.

strategic_ideas: The Slav Defense — Black supports d5 with ...c6 instead of ...e6. The c8 bishop remains free.
Play 4.Nc3 — the main line. Black will likely play ...dxc4 or ...e6.
The Slav is very solid for Black but White maintains a small edge with active development.

Nc3.label: Main Line — 4.Nc3
Nc3.prose: Develops the knight and prepares e4 ideas. After 4...dxc4 5.a4 (preventing ...b5) or 4...e6 5.e3 (solid setup). The Slav leads to rich, strategic play.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Nc3 dxc4 5.a4 Bf5 6.e3 e6 — the Semi-Slav territory. White maintains central tension with good piece activity.

e3.label: Quiet — 4.e3
e3.prose: Solid and unambitious. Keeps the pawn structure intact but gives Black easy equality. Better to play 4.Nc3 for more ambition.
e3.follow_up_idea: After 4.e3 e6 5.Bd3 dxc4 6.Bxc4 — you transpose to QGA-like positions.

strategic_ideas: Your opponent played 1.e4. Your repertoire: 1...c6 (Caro-Kann) or 1...e6 (French Defense).
Caro-Kann is more solid — you support ...d5 with ...c6, keeping the bishop active.
French is slightly more combative — you get a strong pawn chain but your c8-bishop can be passive.

c6.label: Caro-Kann (Your Move)
c6.prose: Your primary defense. Prepares ...d5 while keeping the c8 bishop's diagonal open. After 2.d4 d5 3.Nc3 you enter the Classical Caro-Kann — solid, principled, and popular from 700 to 2700 ELO.
c6.follow_up_idea: After 1...c6 2.d4 d5 — now you've established your pawn duo. White's main systems: 3.Nc3 (Classical), 3.e5 (Advance), 3.exd5 (Exchange). All covered in your repertoire.

e6.label: French Defense (Your Move)
e6.prose: Your secondary defense. Solid but your c8 bishop risks becoming passive. After 2.d4 d5 you challenge the center. Good when you want counterattacking chances rather than solidity.
e6.follow_up_idea: After 1...e6 2.d4 d5 — the French is established. Your choices: Advance (3.e5), Classical (3.Nc3), or Tarrasch (3.Nd2). Each has a different strategic character.

c5.label: Sicilian — Not Your Line
c5.prose: Very combative but requires significant theory. Stick to Caro-Kann or French for now — they suit your style better.
c5.follow_up_idea: If curious: after 1...c5 2.Nf3 d6 3.d4 cxd4 4.Nxd4 you're in the Open Sicilian — very complex. Save this for later.

e5.label: Open Game — Not Your Line
e5.prose: Symmetric center play leads to Ruy Lopez, Italian etc. Rich in theory. Your Caro-Kann and French are better choices for your current level.
e5.follow_up_idea: After 1...e5 2.Nf3 Nc6 White plays 3.Bb5 (Ruy Lopez) or 3.Bc4 (Italian) — both require significant preparation.

strategic_ideas: You've played 1...c6. White's main reply is 2.d4, occupying the center.
After 2.d4 d5 you've established your Caro-Kann pawn duo — fight for the center is on.
If White plays 2.Nc3, you respond 2...d5 anyway.

d4.label: Main Line — 2.d4
d4.prose: The most common and ambitious reply. Play 2...d5 immediately — establish your central pawn.
d4.follow_up_idea: After 2.d4 d5 White has three main systems: 3.Nc3 (Classical), 3.e5 (Advance), 3.exd5 (Exchange). All are in your repertoire.

Nc3.label: Two Knights — 2.Nc3
Nc3.prose: Develops a piece first. Play 2...d5 immediately — the same plan as against 2.d4.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nc3 d5 3.d4 — you've reached the Classical Caro-Kann by transposition. Or 3.Nf3 Bg4 for the Two Knights variation.

Nf3.label: Flexible — 2.Nf3
Nf3.prose: White delays committing. Play 2...d5 anyway — your Caro plan is the same.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nf3 d5 3.exd5 cxd5 — simplified position, easy equality for Black.

strategic_ideas: White has played the ideal 2.d4. Your response is immediate: 2...d5.
After 2...d5 you have a strong pawn duo challenging White's center.
Now White chooses the system: Classical (3.Nc3), Advance (3.e5), or Exchange (3.exd5).

d5.label: Your Move — Essential
d5.prose: Immediately fight for the center. After 2...d5 the Caro-Kann is properly established. This is the only move you should ever consider here.
d5.follow_up_idea: After 2...d5 — watch what White plays. 3.Nc3 = Classical (your main line), 3.e5 = Advance (active counterplay), 3.exd5 = Exchange (easy equality).

strategic_ideas: The Caro-Kann pawn duo is established. Now you react to what White plays.
3.Nc3 (Classical): you play 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 — the main line, very active.
3.e5 (Advance): you play 3...Bf5 — grab the bishop outside the chain before it gets locked in.
3.exd5 (Exchange): you take back 3...cxd5 — symmetric and easy.

Nc3.label: Classical — 3.Nc3
Nc3.prose: The main line. After 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 — you take back the pawn and immediately develop your bishop actively. This is your primary Caro-Kann continuation.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nc3 dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 5.Ng3 Bg6 — both bishops are developed actively. Black is slightly behind in development but very solid.

e5.label: Advance — 3.e5
e5.prose: White grabs space. Your response: 3...Bf5 immediately! Get the bishop outside the pawn chain before playing ...e6 (which would lock it in). This is critical.
e5.follow_up_idea: After 3.e5 Bf5 4.Nf3 e6 5.Be2 c5 — Black is fine. The Advance gives White space but Black has a solid position and can undermine with ...c5.

exd5.label: Exchange — 3.exd5
exd5.prose: White simplifies. After 3...cxd5 you have a symmetric position with easy equality. White has no advantage — this is considered one of the tamest options for White.
exd5.follow_up_idea: After 3.exd5 cxd5 4.Bd3 Nc6 5.c3 Nf6 — develop normally. The symmetrical structure means Black has no problems.

Nd2.label: Rarer — 3.Nd2
Nd2.prose: White develops the knight to d2 instead of c3 — avoids the pin after ...Bb4. Play 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 — same Classical plan as against 3.Nc3.
Nd2.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nd2 dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 5.Ng3 Bg6 — identical structure to the Classical Caro. Your bishop is active on g6.

strategic_ideas: White has recaptured with the knight. NOW play 4...Bf5 immediately — the defining move of the Classical Caro-Kann.
4...Bf5 develops the bishop outside the pawn chain before it gets locked in by ...e6.
This is one of the main reasons the Caro-Kann is better than the French — your bishop is free!

Bf5.label: The Key Move — 4...Bf5
Bf5.prose: Essential! The bishop must come out now before you play ...e6. After 5.Ng3 Bg6 — the bishop retreats to g6 where it stays active. White must now deal with the bishop pair pressure.
Bf5.follow_up_idea: After 4...Bf5 5.Ng3 Bg6 6.h4 h6 7.Nf3 Nd7 8.h5 Bh7 — you've kept the bishop and have a solid, playable position. The Caro-Kann main line.

Nf6.label: Alternative — 4...Nf6
Nf6.prose: Also playable — 4...Nf6 challenges the Ne4 immediately. After 5.Nxf6+ exf6 or 5.Nxf6+ gxf6 the position changes character. Less common than 4...Bf5.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 4...Nf6 5.Nxf6+ exf6 — Black gets the bishop pair and half-open e-file but weakened kingside. This is a different structure from the main Caro.

strategic_ideas: You've played ...Bf5. White's best reply is 5.Ng3 — attacking the bishop.
Your response: 5...Bg6 — retreat the bishop to g6. It's safe there and still active.
After ...Bg6 both sides develop: White plays h4 trying to dislodge the bishop, you play h6 to prevent h5.

Ng3.label: White Attacks the Bishop
Ng3.prose: After 5.Ng3 play 5...Bg6 immediately. Don't be afraid — the bishop is perfectly safe on g6. Your plan: ...e6, ...Nf6 or ...Nd7, develop normally, castle kingside.
Ng3.follow_up_idea: After 5.Ng3 Bg6 6.h4 h6 (essential! — prevents h5 trapping the bishop) 7.Nf3 Nd7 8.h5 Bh7 9.Bd3 Bxd3 10.Qxd3 e6 — solid Caro-Kann position.

strategic_ideas: Your bishop is safely on g6. White will play h4 to pressure it.
Key defensive move: when White plays h4, you MUST play h6 immediately to prevent h5 trapping the bishop.
After the h4-h6 sequence, play ...e6, ...Nd7, and castle. You have a solid, respectable position.

h4.label: Attack the Bishop — 6.h4
h4.prose: White probes your bishop. After 6.h4 play 6...h6 immediately — essential! This prevents h5 which would trap your bishop. After 6...h6 7.Nf3 Nd7 — continue developing.
h4.follow_up_idea: After 6.h4 h6 7.Nf3 Nd7 8.h5 Bh7 9.Bd3 Bxd3 10.Qxd3 e6 11.Bf4 Ngf6 — you're in the main Classical Caro-Kann. Slightly passive but very solid.

Nf3.label: Develop First — 6.Nf3
Nf3.prose: White develops instead. Play 6...Nf6 or 6...Nd7 — develop naturally. You have a comfortable game.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 6.Nf3 Nd7 7.Bd3 e6 8.0-0 Ngf6 — solid Caro structure, you'll castle soon.

strategic_ideas: White plays the Advance Variation (3.e5) — grabbing space. Your response is CRITICAL.
Play 3...Bf5 IMMEDIATELY — before playing ...e6. This is the entire point of the Caro-Kann vs the French.
If you play ...e6 first, your bishop gets locked in like in the French — exactly what you were trying to avoid.

Bf5.label: CRITICAL — Must Play Immediately
Bf5.prose: 3...Bf5 is the most important move in the Advance Caro. The bishop is developed before the pawn chain closes. After 4.Nf3 e6 5.Be2 c5 — you have a solid position with active pieces.
Bf5.follow_up_idea: After 3...Bf5 4.Nf3 e6 5.Be2 c5 6.0-0 Nc6 — Black is completely fine. The Advance Caro is not dangerous if you remember to play ...Bf5 first.

strategic_ideas: You've played ...Bf5 — the bishop is safely developed. White must now choose a plan.
White's main ideas: Nf3 (developing), Nc3 (aggressive but risky after ...e6), c3 (supporting d4).
Your plan: ...e6, ...Nd7, ...Ngf6, ...Be7 and castle. Black is comfortable.

Nf3.label: Normal — 4.Nf3
Nf3.prose: White develops normally. Play 4...e6 5.Be2 c5 — undermine the pawn chain immediately. After 6.0-0 Nc6 you have a solid, comfortable Caro-Kann.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Nf3 e6 5.Be2 c5 6.0-0 Nc6 7.c3 Nge7 — Black's position is solid. The ...c5 break will eventually equalize.

Nc3.label: Aggressive — 4.Nc3
Nc3.prose: Developing but also preparing f4. After 4...e6 (solid) or 4...a6 (preventing Nb5) Black has good chances.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Nc3 e6 5.g4 Bg6 6.Nge2 — the aggressive g4 push. Respond with 6...c5 undermining the center.

c3.label: Solid — 4.c3
c3.prose: White fortifies d4. Play 4...e6 and develop normally. Black has no problems.
c3.follow_up_idea: After 4.c3 e6 5.Nf3 Nd7 6.Be2 Ne7 — Black will castle and eventually break with ...c5.

strategic_ideas: The Exchange Variation — White simplifies with 3.exd5 and you recapture 3...cxd5.
This is the easiest variation for Black. The position is symmetric; you have no problems.
White's only plan is a minority attack (b4-b5) on the queenside. Easy to handle.

Bd3.label: Typical — 4.Bd3
Bd3.prose: White develops the bishop. Play 4...Nc6 5.c3 Nf6 6.Nf3 Bg4 — develop actively. You can also play 4...Nf6 immediately.
Bd3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Bd3 Nc6 5.c3 Nf6 6.Bf4 — White plans Nd2-Nf3 and minority attack. You play ...Bg4, ...e6, and castle. Comfortable equality.

c4.label: Aggressive — 4.c4
c4.prose: White immediately challenges d5 — entering Panov Attack territory. Play 4...Nf6 5.Nc3 e6 — the Semi-Panov. Solid and active for Black.
c4.follow_up_idea: After 4.c4 Nf6 5.Nc3 e6 6.Nf3 Be7 — you're in a Queen's Gambit-like structure. Black is fine.

Nc3.label: Development — 4.Nc3
Nc3.prose: White develops. Play 4...Nf6 5.Bf4 Nc6 — active development. Black has easy equality.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Nc3 Nf6 5.Bf4 Nc6 6.Nf3 e6 — typical symmetrical structure. Castle kingside and equalize.

strategic_ideas: White plays 3.Nc3 — the Classical and Winawer French. You have two main choices.
3...Bb4 = Winawer Variation — pins the knight, creates imbalance, very popular and sharp.
3...Nf6 = Classical French — develops naturally, leads to rich strategic play.

Bb4.label: Winawer — Sharp and Ambitious
Bb4.prose: The Winawer is Black's most combative choice. You pin the Nc3, create an imbalance, and fight for the initiative. After 4.e5 c5 you immediately attack White's center.
Bb4.follow_up_idea: After 3...Bb4 4.e5 c5 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.bxc3 Ne7 7.Qg4 (the main line) — Black plays 7...Qc7 and the game is very sharp. White attacks the kingside, Black attacks the center.

Nf6.label: Classical — Solid Development
Nf6.prose: Develop the knight and maintain tension. After 4.e5 (Steinitz Variation) Nfd7 5.f4 c5 6.Nf3 Nc6 — Black has a solid French setup.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 3...Nf6 4.Bg5 (McCutcheon) or 4.e5 Nfd7 5.f4 — the French Classical leads to complex pawn structures. Black will play ...c5 to undermine White's center.

dxe4.label: Rubinstein — 3...dxe4
dxe4.prose: Black releases the tension early by capturing. After 4.Nxe4 Nd7 5.Nf3 Ngf6 — the Rubinstein French. Solid and unambitious.
dxe4.follow_up_idea: After 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 Nd7 5.Nf3 Ngf6 6.Nxf6+ Nxf6 — symmetrical development. Black has no problems but also no advantage.

strategic_ideas: The Winawer is pinned — your bishop targets the Nc3. White will likely play 4.e5 grabbing space.
After 4.e5 play 4...c5 immediately — attack the pawn chain at its base.
This is a principled approach: White goes for the kingside, you counterattack in the center.

e5.label: Main Line — 4.e5
e5.prose: White grabs space. Play 4...c5 immediately — the Winawer main line. You attack d4 while White attacks the kingside. Sharp and unbalanced.
e5.follow_up_idea: After 4.e5 c5 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.bxc3 Ne7 7.Qg4 (Qg4 is White's most dangerous) — you must play 7...Qc7! (not 7...0-0? 8.Bh6). The position is very sharp.

a3.label: Immediate Exchange — 4.a3
a3.prose: White forces the exchange immediately. After 4...Bxc3+ 5.bxc3 dxe4 6.Qg4 — the Winawer Exchange. Play 6...Nf6! 7.Qxg7 Rg8 8.Qh6 Rg6 — Black gets counterplay.
a3.follow_up_idea: After 4.a3 Bxc3+ 5.bxc3 dxe4 — Black has the bishop pair and White's c-pawn structure is damaged. The position is balanced but complex.

strategic_ideas: White has played e5 — grabbing kingside space. Your counter: attack the pawn chain.
4...c5 is your move — immediately attack d4. This is the standard Winawer plan.
The game will be sharp: White attacks on the kingside, you counterattack in the center and queenside.

c5.label: Attack the Base — Essential
c5.prose: 4...c5 is the defining move of the Winawer. Attack d4 immediately. After 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.bxc3 Ne7 7.Qg4 you must play 7...Qc7 — protects g7 and maintains your counterattack.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 4...c5 5.a3 Bxc3+ 6.bxc3 Ne7 7.Qg4 Qc7 8.Qxg7 Rg8 9.Qxh7 cxd4 — both sides are attacking. Black has two pawns for the exchange and active counterplay.

strategic_ideas: You've played 3...Nf6 — classical development. White's main choices: 4.e5 (Steinitz) or 4.Bg5 (Classical).
After 4.e5 Nfd7 5.f4 — the Steinitz Variation. Black attacks with ...c5.
After 4.Bg5 — the Classical French with the bishop pin. Very theoretical.

e5.label: Steinitz — 4.e5
e5.prose: White grabs space and attacks your knight. Retreat with 4...Nfd7 (not Nxe4? which loses material). Then ...c5 to undermine the center.
e5.follow_up_idea: After 4.e5 Nfd7 5.f4 c5 6.Nf3 Nc6 7.Be3 — standard Steinitz French. Black attacks d4 and e5, White defends and tries to maintain the pawn chain.

Bg5.label: Classical Pin — 4.Bg5
Bg5.prose: White pins the knight. This is the McCutcheon if you play 4...Bb4. Classical if you play 4...Be7.
Bg5.follow_up_idea: After 4.Bg5 Be7 5.e5 Nfd7 6.Bxe7 Qxe7 — the Classical French. Or 4...Bb4 5.e5 h6 6.Bxf6 gxf6 — the McCutcheon, very complex.

strategic_ideas: White plays the Advance Variation — 3.e5 grabs space immediately.
Your key move: 3...c5 — attack the pawn chain at its base. Do this soon.
Also possible: 3...Bd7 (preparing ...Bb5) but 3...c5 is more direct.

c5.label: Attack the Chain — Main Line
c5.prose: 3...c5 is your primary weapon against the Advance. Attack d4 immediately. After 4.c3 Nc6 5.Nf3 Qb6 — you put immediate pressure on d4 and b2.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 3...c5 4.c3 Nc6 5.Nf3 Qb6 6.Bd3 cxd4 7.cxd4 Bd7 — Black has active piece play and pressure against White's center. The b2 pawn is often vulnerable.

Bd7.label: Quiet — 3...Bd7
Bd7.prose: Preparing ...Bb5 or ...Ne7. Solid but less ambitious. After 4.Nf3 Bb5 5.c3 — interesting maneuvering.
Bd7.follow_up_idea: After 3...Bd7 4.Nf3 Bb5 5.c3 Ne7 — the Euwe Variation. Solid but Black's pieces are a bit passive.

strategic_ideas: You've played ...c5 — the principled counter. White usually plays 4.c3 to support d4.
Your follow-up: ...Nc6 (develop and add pressure to d4) and ...Qb6 (attack b2).
The French Advance is a battle: White's space vs your piece activity.

c3.label: Main — 4.c3
c3.prose: White supports d4. Play 4...Nc6 5.Nf3 Qb6 — the main line. Your queen attacks b2 and d4. After 6.Bd3 cxd4 7.cxd4 Bd7 you have active play.
c3.follow_up_idea: After 4.c3 Nc6 5.Nf3 Qb6 6.Bd3 cxd4 7.cxd4 Nxd4 8.Nxd4 Qxd4 9.Nf3! — White gets the two bishops in an endgame, but Black is solid.

Nf3.label: Development — 4.Nf3
Nf3.prose: White develops. Play 4...Nc6 and continue your plan. After 5.c3 Qb6 — same idea as main line.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Nf3 Nc6 5.c3 Qb6 6.Bd3 cxd4 7.cxd4 — same Advance structures apply.

strategic_ideas: White plays the Tarrasch (3.Nd2) — avoiding the Winawer pin. A solid, positional choice.
Your options: 3...Nf6 (classical development) or 3...c5 (immediate counterattack).
The Tarrasch is considered equal with correct play. Black has several good systems.

Nf6.label: Classical — 3...Nf6
Nf6.prose: Natural development. After 4.e5 Nfd7 5.Bd3 c5 6.c3 Nc6 — standard French Tarrasch structure. Black attacks the center with ...c5.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 3...Nf6 4.e5 Nfd7 5.Bd3 c5 6.c3 Nc6 7.Ne2 — White is solid. Black will play ...cxd4, ...f6 to break up the center.

c5.label: Immediate Counter — 3...c5
c5.prose: Attack at once. After 4.exd5 exd5 5.dxc5 (or 5.Ngf3) — play 5...Bxc5 and develop actively. Or after 4.exd5 Qxd5 5.Ngf3 cxd4 6.Bc4 — Black has good piece activity.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 3...c5 4.exd5 exd5 5.Ngf3 Nc6 6.Bb5 Bd6 — the Guimard Variation. Black has easy development and the open e-file.

Be7.label: Solid — 3...Be7
Be7.prose: Very solid. Prepares kingside castling quickly. After 4.Ngf3 Nf6 5.e5 Nfd7 — standard Tarrasch.
Be7.follow_up_idea: After 3...Be7 4.Ngf3 Nf6 5.e5 Nfd7 6.Bd3 c5 — Black's plan is the same: attack the center with ...c5.

strategic_ideas: White plays the Exchange French (3.exd5 exd5). Symmetric and relatively drawish.
The Exchange is White's least ambitious choice. You should have no problems.
Develop naturally: ...Nf6, ...Bd6, ...0-0. The position is balanced.

Bd3.label: Standard — 4.Bd3
Bd3.prose: Play 4...Nf6 5.Nf3 Bd6 — symmetric development. Castle kingside and equalize easily.
Bd3.follow_up_idea: After 4.Bd3 Nf6 5.Nf3 Bd6 6.0-0 0-0 7.Re1 Re8 — completely equal. White has a small initiative but Black is solid.

c4.label: Aggressive — 4.c4
c4.prose: White enters Panov-like territory. After 4...Nf6 5.Nc3 Bb4 — the Nimzo-Indian-like setup. Or 4...c6 for solidity.
c4.follow_up_idea: After 4.c4 dxc4 5.Bxc4 Nf6 6.Nf3 — White has a slight development lead but Black is fine.

strategic_ideas: The French pawn duo (e6+d5) is established. Now White chooses their system.
3.Nc3 (Classical/Winawer), 3.Nd2 (Tarrasch), 3.e5 (Advance) — all require different Black responses.
Your key adaptations: vs Nc3 → Winawer (Bb4) or Classical (Nf6); vs e5 → c5 attack; vs Nd2 → Nf6 or c5.

Nc3.label: Classical/Winawer — 3.Nc3
Nc3.prose: Most popular. Choose: 3...Bb4 (Winawer — sharp, ambitious) or 3...Nf6 (Classical — solid). Both are good at your level.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: Winawer after 3...Bb4 4.e5 c5 — you attack immediately. Classical after 3...Nf6 4.e5 Nfd7 5.f4 c5 — slower but solid.

Nd2.label: Tarrasch — 3.Nd2
Nd2.prose: Avoids the Winawer pin. Play 3...Nf6 or 3...c5. Both lead to good French positions.
Nd2.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nd2 Nf6 4.e5 Nfd7 5.Bd3 c5 — standard Tarrasch. Attack the center with ...c5.

e5.label: Advance — 3.e5
e5.prose: White grabs space. Play 3...c5 immediately — attack the pawn chain. Don't wait.
e5.follow_up_idea: After 3.e5 c5 4.c3 Nc6 5.Nf3 Qb6 — pressure on b2 and d4. Your main French Advance line.

exd5.label: Exchange — 3.exd5
exd5.prose: Harmless. After 3...exd5 you have a symmetric position with easy equality.
exd5.follow_up_idea: After 3.exd5 exd5 — develop normally with Nf6, Bd6, 0-0. White has no real advantage.

strategic_ideas: You've played 1...e6 (French Defense). Now you must play ...d5 on move 2 regardless of what White does.
After 2.d4 d5 — the French is properly established.
If White plays 2.Nf3 or 2.Nc3 first, you still play 2...d5.

d4.label: Main Line — 2.d4
d4.prose: The standard response. Play 2...d5 — establish the French pawn duo. The real game starts now.
d4.follow_up_idea: After 2.d4 d5 — watch for 3.Nc3 (Classical), 3.e5 (Advance), or 3.Nd2 (Tarrasch). All covered in your repertoire.

Nf3.label: Flexible — 2.Nf3
Nf3.prose: White develops first. Still play 2...d5 — the same French plan.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nf3 d5 3.Nc3 — Classical French. Or 3.e5 Nfd7 — Advance with knight deployed early.

Nc3.label: Two Knights — 2.Nc3
Nc3.prose: Play 2...d5 — the Two Knights French. After 3.d4 — normal French. Or 3.Nf3 — flexible.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nc3 d5 3.d4 — Classical French positions by transposition.

strategic_ideas: White develops the bishop directly to f4 on move 2 — the direct London move order.
This is equally valid — the bishop reaches f4 whether you play Nf3 first or not.
Black will likely play ...Nf6 and then ...e6, entering normal London territory.

Nf6.label: Normal Development
Nf6.prose: Black develops normally. Play 3.Nf3 and you're in standard London. After 3...e6 4.e3 — normal London structure.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 2.Bf4 Nf6 3.Nf3 e6 4.e3 — standard London System. Continue with Bd3, Nbd2, c3, 0-0.

c5.label: Aggressive Counter
c5.prose: Black challenges the center early. Play 3.e3 and continue developing. Don't panic.
c5.follow_up_idea: After 2.Bf4 c5 3.e3 Nc6 4.Nf3 Nf6 5.c3 — solid London setup is maintained.

strategic_ideas: White plays Nf3 before committing to Bg5 or Bf4. Solid and flexible.
After 4.Nf3 Be7 5.Bg5 — you reach the same Classical QGD positions.
Black continues developing naturally: ...Be7, ...0-0, ...Nbd7.

Be7.label: Solid — 4...Be7
Be7.prose: Develop solidly and prepare to castle. After 5.Bg5 0-0 6.e3 — normal Classical QGD. You'll play ...Nbd7 and ...c6 for a solid setup.
Be7.follow_up_idea: After 4...Be7 5.e3 0-0 6.Bd3 dxc4 7.Bxc4 c5 — the Tartakower plan. Solid and good for Black.

dxc4.label: QGA Approach — 4...dxc4
dxc4.prose: Accept the gambit to change the pawn structure. After 5.e3 a6 6.Bxc4 b5 — the Meran/Semi-Slav type positions.
dxc4.follow_up_idea: After 4...dxc4 5.e3 a6 6.Bxc4 b5 7.Bd3 — the game becomes more dynamic with Black's queenside expansion.

strategic_ideas: The main Slav position. White has Nc3 and Nf3 developed, Black has the Slav structure (c6+d5).
Black's main ideas: 4...dxc4 (Semi-Slav setup) or 4...e6 (more solid).
The Slav is very solid — Black's c8 bishop has the b7 diagonal available (unlike QGD).

dxc4.label: Semi-Slav — 4...dxc4
dxc4.prose: Black grabs the pawn. After 5.a4 (preventing ...b5) Bf5 6.e3 e6 — the Semi-Slav. Black will try to keep the c4 pawn with ...b5 eventually.
dxc4.follow_up_idea: After 4...dxc4 5.a4 Bf5 6.e3 e6 7.Bxc4 Bb4 — Botvinnik Semi-Slav territory. Very sharp and theoretical.

e6.label: Solid — 4...e6
e6.prose: Solid and safe. Black keeps the pawn structure intact. After 5.e3 Nbd7 6.Bd3 — normal Slav development.
e6.follow_up_idea: After 4...e6 5.e3 Nbd7 6.Bd3 dxc4 7.Bxc4 b5 — the Meran Variation. Dynamic and well-tested.

strategic_ideas: White plays 2.Nc3 before d4 — the Two Knights Caro-Kann. You've correctly played 2...d5.
White will likely follow with 3.d4 (transposing to Classical) or 3.Nf3.
Your plan is the same: establish the d5 pawn and develop actively.

d4.label: Transposes to Classical
d4.prose: After 3.d4 — you're in a Classical Caro-Kann by transposition. Now White plays 3.exd5 or 3.Nc3-then-e5. Your plans are identical.
d4.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nc3 d5 3.d4 — Classical Caro-Kann positions. Watch for 4.e5 (Advance) or 4.exd5 (Exchange).

Nf3.label: Two Knights Development
Nf3.prose: 3.Nf3 develops normally. Play 3...Bg4 (pinning) or 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 Nf6 — active development.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nc3 d5 3.Nf3 Bg4 — the Two Knights Caro. White must decide how to handle the pin.

strategic_ideas: The Classical Caro-Kann! White has both center pawns supported by the knight.
Your main move: 3...dxe4 — take the pawn and develop actively. Don't play 3...Nf6 (gets kicked by 4.e5).
After 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 — the Classical Caro-Kann proper begins.

dxe4.label: Main Line — 3...dxe4
dxe4.prose: The principled move — take the pawn and force White to recapture with the knight. After 4.Nxe4 Bf5 (the key move!) — you develop the bishop actively.
dxe4.follow_up_idea: After 3...dxe4 4.Nxe4 Bf5 5.Ng3 Bg6 — both your bishops are active. This is the Classical Caro-Kann main line.

Nf6.label: Risky — 3...Nf6
Nf6.prose: After 4.e5 Ne4 5.Nxe4 dxe4 — you have the bishop pair but structural weakness. Not recommended at your level.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 3...Nf6 4.e5 Nfd7 — a different kind of French-like position. Possible but the Classical (3...dxe4) is more active.

strategic_ideas: You've captured on e4. White recaptures with 4.Nxe4 — the knight comes to a very active square.
NOW play 4...Bf5 immediately — this is your critical move. Don't delay it.
The bishop on f5 is developed, active, and outside the pawn chain — the Caro-Kann advantage.

Nxe4.label: Standard Recapture
Nxe4.prose: After 4.Nxe4 — PLAY 4...Bf5 IMMEDIATELY. This is the defining move of the Classical Caro-Kann. Get the bishop out now before playing ...e6.
Nxe4.follow_up_idea: After 4.Nxe4 Bf5 5.Ng3 Bg6 — bishop safely to g6, then ...e6, ...Nd7, ...Ngf6, castle. Solid and active.

strategic_ideas: If you play 4...Nf6 instead of 4...Bf5, White can exchange on f6 or let you challenge.
4...Bf5 is stronger and more principled — always prefer it.
After 4...Nf6 5.Nxf6+ gxf6 — Black's kingside is damaged. Or 5.Nxf6+ exf6 — doubled pawns.

Nxf6+.label: White Exchanges — 5.Nxf6+
Nxf6+.prose: After 5.Nxf6+ exf6 (take with pawn to open e-file) — Black has active piece play but damaged pawn structure. Or 5...gxf6 — even more damaged but two bishops.
Nxf6+.follow_up_idea: Remember: 4...Bf5 is better than 4...Nf6 in the Caro-Kann Classical. Always play Bf5 first when the knight recaptures on e4.

strategic_ideas: Black plays 1...e5 — the Englund Gambit. Very common at 200-600 ELO. Black offers a pawn hoping for tricks.
TAKE THE PAWN: 2.dxe5 — always capture. This is sound and leaves Black with a worse position.
Don't play 2.d5 (gives Black free space) or 2.e3 (ignores the challenge). Just take.

dxe5.label: Correct — Take the Pawn
dxe5.prose: 2.dxe5 is the right move. After 2...Nc6 3.Nf3 (or 3.Bf4) Black tries to win back the pawn, but White is simply up a pawn with no compensation given to Black. Don't be afraid of tricks — they're manageable.
dxe5.follow_up_idea: After 2.dxe5 Nc6 3.Nf3 Qe7 4.Bf4 — defend the pawn, develop pieces. Black's compensation is minimal. You are simply a pawn up.

d5.label: Mistake — Don't Do This
d5.prose: 2.d5 closes the center and gives Black free space. After 2...Bc5 or 2...d6 Black has a perfectly playable Benoni-like position. You've missed the chance to win material.
d5.follow_up_idea: Avoid 2.d5 — always capture on e5 when given the chance.

strategic_ideas: You've taken the pawn. Now hold it! Black's main move is 2...Nc6 trying to win it back.
Key plan: don't give it back without compensation. Play Nf3, Bf4, and develop solidly.
Black has NO theoretical compensation for the pawn — just play normally and you'll be better.

Nc6.label: Gambit Accepted — 2...Nc6
Nc6.prose: The main line. After 3.Nf3 Qe7 4.Bf4 — protect the e5 pawn calmly. If 4...Nxe5 5.Nxe5 Qxe5 6.Bxe5 — Black has won back the pawn but White has a big development lead.
Nc6.follow_up_idea: After 2...Nc6 3.Nf3 Qe7 4.Qd5! (controlling e5 from a safe square) — White simply holds the pawn and develops. Black's attack has no punch.

d6.label: Delayed Recapture
d6.prose: 3.Nf3 and develop. After 3...dxe5 4.Nxe5 — you've returned the pawn but with better development. Or just hold with 3.Bf4.
d6.follow_up_idea: After 2...d6 3.exd6 Bxd6 4.Nf3 — you've traded pawns but your development is faster.

strategic_ideas: Black plays 2...Nc6 — pressuring the e5 pawn. Black has tricks up their sleeve at low ELO.
Critical: after 3.Nf3 Qe7 Black threatens 4...Qb4+ fork or 4...Nxe5. You must be alert.
Best: 3.Nf3 Qe7 4.Qd5! — centralizes your queen and holds the pawn. Or 4.Bf4.

Nf3.label: Develop and Defend
Nf3.prose: 3.Nf3 develops and guards e5. After 3...Qe7 4.Bf4 Nxe5? 5.Nxe5 Qxe5 6.Bxe5 — you win a queen for two minor pieces! This is a common beginner trap Black sets. Don't fall for it backwards.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nf3 Qe7 4.Bf4 — White is simply better with an extra pawn and good development. Play c3, Be2 and castle. The pawn on e5 is a target but it holds.

Bf4.label: Direct Defense
Bf4.prose: 3.Bf4 defends the pawn and develops. After 3...Qe7 4.Nc3 Nxe5 5.Nxe5 Qxe5 6.Bxe5 — White wins the queen again!
Bf4.follow_up_idea: After 3.Bf4 Qe7 4.Nc3 — solid. Develop and castle. Black cannot win back the pawn without losing material.

strategic_ideas: The Dutch Defense — Black grabs kingside space with a flank pawn. Common at 400-700 ELO.
Your London System still works here: 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 — the setup is identical.
The Dutch bishop on f5 is awkward compared to your bishop on f4 — you have the easier game.

Nf3.label: London Setup (Recommended)
Nf3.prose: 2.Nf3 and London setup is perfect against the Dutch. After 2...Nf6 3.Bf4 — you have a comfortable London while Black's f5 pawn just weakens the kingside without creating real counterplay.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 Be7 5.Bd3 — solid London. Black's f5 pawn looks aggressive but you can target it later with Ne5 or e4 ideas.

Bg5.label: Anti-Dutch — 2.Bg5
Bg5.prose: A tricky anti-Dutch weapon. After 2...h6 3.Bh4 g5 4.e3 — Black has weakened their kingside significantly trying to keep the bishop. Very playable.
Bg5.follow_up_idea: After 2.Bg5 h6 3.Bh4 g5 4.e3 gxh4 5.Qh5+ — White wins material! This is a common beginner trap in the Dutch. But 2.Nf3 London is more reliable.

c4.label: Queen's Gambit vs Dutch
c4.prose: 2.c4 is main-line chess against the Dutch. After 2...Nf6 3.Nc3 e6 — Stonewall Dutch territory. Complex and theoretical.
c4.follow_up_idea: 2.c4 is fine but 2.Nf3 London is more practical for your repertoire.

strategic_ideas: Your London bishop is on f4 — your setup is complete against the Dutch too.
Key difference vs normal London: Black's f5 pawn means they can't easily play ...e6 without blocking the bishop.
Your plan: e3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3, castle. Black will struggle to develop the c8 bishop.

e6.label: Stonewall Setup
e6.prose: Black builds a pawn wall with ...e6 and ...d5. After 4.e3 Be7 5.Bd3 d5 6.0-0 — you're in London vs Stonewall Dutch. Black's f5/e6/d5 triangle is solid but passive.
e6.follow_up_idea: Vs Stonewall Dutch: play c4 to break the pawn chain, or Ne5 to target the weakness on e6. Black's c8 bishop is permanently restricted.

g6.label: Leningrad Dutch
g6.prose: Black fianchettoes the kingside bishop. After 4.e3 Bg7 5.Bd3 d6 6.0-0 — Leningrad Dutch vs London. Your solid setup gives you easy play.
g6.follow_up_idea: In Leningrad vs London: c4 is your space-gaining move. Black's kingside fianchetto is less effective when their f5 pawn is loose.

strategic_ideas: Black pins your Nf3 with 2...Bg4 — a common trick at 400-700 ELO. They plan ...e6, ...Nd7 and your knight can't easily escape.
Calm response: 3.Ne5 (attacking the bishop immediately) or 3.Bf4 (ignore the pin and develop).
Don't panic — the pin is annoying but not dangerous. Your London structure is fine.

Ne5.label: Attack the Bishop — 3.Ne5
Ne5.prose: 3.Ne5! attacks the Bg4 immediately. After 3...Bh5 4.Bf4 — you've gained a tempo and developed both pieces. If 3...Bxd1 4.Bb5+ wins material for White.
Ne5.follow_up_idea: After 3.Ne5 Bh5 4.Bf4 e6 5.e3 Bd6 6.Nd3 — your pieces are active and coordinated. The London setup is healthy.

h3.label: Ask the Bishop — 3.h3
h3.prose: Force Black to decide: 3...Bh5 (maintains pin but gives you g4 ideas) or 3...Bxf3 4.Qxf3 (you get the queen pair but Black equalizes). 3.h3 is solid if you're not sure.
h3.follow_up_idea: After 3.h3 Bxf3 4.Qxf3 — you have the bishop pair but Black has a solid position. After 4.exf3 you get a half-open e-file and can play the London as normal.

Bf4.label: Ignore the Pin — 3.Bf4
Bf4.prose: Just develop! After 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 — the pin is just a pin. Your London works fine with the knight pinned. Later you can play h3 to drive the bishop away.
Bf4.follow_up_idea: After 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 Nd7 5.h3 Bh5 6.Bd3 — solid London. The pin creates no real problems for you.

strategic_ideas: Black plays 2...Nc6 — not a theoretical move but common at 200-500 ELO. The knight doesn't belong on c6 in a d4 structure.
Just develop normally: 3.Bf4 and play London. The Nc6 will be misplaced after you establish your pawn center.
This is simply a mistake by Black — play normally and you'll be better.

Bf4.label: London as Planned
Bf4.prose: 3.Bf4 and continue with e3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3, castle. Black's Nc6 is fine in a 1.e4 e5 game but here the knight blocks ...c5 or ...c6 and has no ideal square.
Bf4.follow_up_idea: After 3.Bf4 e6 4.e3 Nf6 5.Bd3 — Black's pieces don't coordinate well. The Nc6 blocks the c-pawn and doesn't fight for key squares in this structure.

e4.label: Aggressive — 3.e4!?
e4.prose: You could also play 3.e4!? immediately since Black's Nc6 doesn't help control e4. After 3...dxe4 4.Ng5 Nf6 5.Nc3 — active play for White.
e4.follow_up_idea: Simpler to just play London with 3.Bf4 — don't complicate when you have a good system.

strategic_ideas: Black plays 1...d6 — a flexible move leading to Pirc or King's Indian structures. Common at 300-600 ELO.
Your London System still works: 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 or 2.Nf3 g6 3.Bf4 Bg7 4.e3.
Black will fianchetto the kingside bishop. Your solid center gives you a comfortable advantage.

Nf3.label: London Setup (Recommended)
Nf3.prose: 2.Nf3 and after 2...Nf6 3.Bf4 or 2...g6 3.Bf4 Bg7 4.e3 — London works fine. Black's Pirc setup is flexible but your solid center is very comfortable.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nf3 Nf6 3.Bf4 g6 4.e3 Bg7 5.Bd3 0-0 6.0-0 — London vs Pirc. Black's kingside fianchetto is fine but White's solid center gives easy play.

e4.label: Classical Response — 2.e4
e4.prose: Enter classical Pirc/Modern territory. After 2.e4 Nf6 3.Nc3 g6 4.f4 — Four Pawns Attack. Very aggressive but lots of theory.
e4.follow_up_idea: 2.Nf3 London is simpler and more reliable for your style.

strategic_ideas: Black immediately fianchettoes — the Modern/Hippo Defense. Common at all ELO levels.
Your London System: 2.Nf3 Bg7 3.Bf4 d6 4.e3 — works perfectly. Black's bishop on g7 is powerful but your solid center neutralizes it.
Don't let Black play ...e5 freely — use your d4 pawn as an anchor.

Nf3.label: London Setup
Nf3.prose: 2.Nf3 Bg7 3.Bf4 d6 4.e3 Nf6 5.Bd3 0-0 6.0-0 — London vs Modern. Your solid center is fine. Black's g7 bishop looks scary but it bites on granite against your London structure.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nf3 Bg7 3.Bf4 d6 4.e3 Nd7 5.Bd3 Ngf6 6.0-0 — prevent ...e5 by playing Ne5 or c3. If Black plays ...e5, exchange on e5 and you have easy play.

e4.label: Classical — 2.e4
e4.prose: After 2.e4 Bg7 3.Nc3 d6 — Modern Defense proper. Rich in theory. Your London is simpler.
e4.follow_up_idea: Stick with 2.Nf3 London — it's your system and works fine here.

strategic_ideas: Black plays 1...Nc6 — the Mikenas Defense. Very unusual, mostly seen at 200-400 ELO.
Just play 2.Nf3 and develop your London. Black's Nc6 will have to go somewhere awkward.
After 2...d5 3.Bf4 — normal London. After 2...e5 — Englund Gambit: take with 3.dxe5.

Nf3.label: Develop Normally
Nf3.prose: 2.Nf3 and see what Black does. If 2...d5 3.Bf4 — London. If 2...e5 3.dxe5 — Englund Gambit (take it). If 2...Nf6 3.Bf4 — London as usual.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: The Mikenas is not a good opening for Black. Just play your London system and you'll have an easy game.

entries.variation_name: 1.e4 c6 2.Bc4 — Unusual but Common at Low ELO
strategic_ideas: White plays 2.Bc4 against your Caro-Kann — the Bishop's Opening. Common at 200-500 ELO.
Your response: 2...d5! immediately. Attack the center and challenge the bishop.
After 2...d5 the bishop on c4 becomes misplaced — it must move or be captured.

d5.label: Correct — 2...d5!
d5.prose: Challenge the center immediately. After 3.exd5 cxd5 4.Bb5+ Bd7 5.Bxd7+ Nxd7 — you've traded off the annoying bishop and have a solid position. Or 3.Bb3 d4 — the bishop retreats and you've gained space.
d5.follow_up_idea: After 2...d5 3.exd5 cxd5 4.Bb5+ Bd7 5.Bxd7+ Qxd7 — you have the bishop pair and a solid pawn center. Develop normally with ...Nf6, ...e6, castle.

e6.label: Passive — 2...e6
e6.prose: Overly cautious. White's bishop on c4 becomes powerful after 3.d4 d5 4.e5 — French-like position but with White's bishop on a better square. Play 2...d5 instead.
e6.follow_up_idea: Always play 2...d5 against 2.Bc4 — challenge the center and make the bishop retreat.

strategic_ideas: You played 2...d5 — excellent. The bishop on c4 is under attack and must retreat or capture.
If 3.exd5 cxd5 4.Bb5+ — simple development equalizes easily.
If 3.Bb3 — the bishop retreats and you've gained a free tempo.
You're in great shape — the Bishop's Opening is harmless against your Caro setup.

exd5.label: Exchange — 3.exd5
exd5.prose: After 3.exd5 cxd5 4.Bb5+ Bd7 5.Bxd7+ Nxd7 — or 4.Bb3 and White has no advantage. Play 4...Nf6 5.d3 e6 — solid and easy development. You're fine.
exd5.follow_up_idea: After 3.exd5 cxd5 — develop with Nf6, e6, Bd6 (or Be7) and castle. The Bishop's Opening poses no problems after you play ...d5.

Bb3.label: Retreat — 3.Bb3
Bb3.prose: White retreats and you've won a tempo! Play 3...dxe4 4.Qh5 Nf6 5.Qe2 e5 — or simply 3...Nf6 4.d3 e5 — excellent position.
Bb3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Bb3 Nf6 4.d3 e5 — you have the center. White's bishop on b3 is just a spectator.

e5.label: Advance — 3.e5
e5.prose: White grabs space but the bishop on c4 is now awkward. After 3...Bf5 4.d3 e6 — solid. Or 3...c5 4.Bb3 — counterattack.
e5.follow_up_idea: After 3.e5 Bf5 4.d3 e6 — similar to Advance Caro-Kann but White's c4 bishop is misplaced. Black is fine.

strategic_ideas: White plays the Wayward Queen (2.Qh5) — very common at 200-500 ELO. Looks scary, isn't.
Your response: 2...g6! — chase the queen away. Do NOT play 2...e6? (2.Qxe5+ forks) or 2...d5? (3.exd5 and Qxe5+ fork).
After 2...g6 3.Qf3 (must retreat) — White has wasted a move and you develop normally.

g6.label: CORRECT — 2...g6!
g6.prose: Chase the queen! After 3.Qf3 (forced retreat — if 3.Qxh8?? Nf6 and White's queen is trapped) 3...d5 4.exd5 cxd5 — you've gained a tempo and have a great center. White has wasted TWO moves with the queen.
g6.follow_up_idea: After 2...g6 3.Qf3 d5 4.exd5 cxd5 5.d4 Nf6 — you've developed and have a solid Caro-Kann structure. White's queen has done nothing useful.

e6.label: MISTAKE — 2...e6?
e6.prose: DANGER! After 2...e6?? 3.Qxe6+! fxe6? 4.— wait, that doesn't win. Actually 2...e6 3.Qe2 is just weird. But avoid it — play 2...g6 to directly attack the queen.
e6.follow_up_idea: Always play 2...g6 against the Wayward Queen. Chase it away and develop your pieces.

d5.label: MISTAKE — 2...d5?
d5.prose: After 2...d5? 3.exd5 cxd5 4.Qxd5! — White picks up the pawn and threatens Qxf7! Don't play d5 while the queen is on h5.
d5.follow_up_idea: 2...g6 first! Only after the queen retreats should you play ...d5.

strategic_ideas: White plays 2.f4 — attempting a King's Gambit-style setup. Unusual but seen at low ELO.
Your Caro-Kann plan still works: 2...d5! — challenge the center immediately.
After 2...d5 3.e5 (Advance-like) 3...Bf5 — you're in Caro-Kann Advance territory with an extra tempo.

d5.label: Correct — 2...d5
d5.prose: Fight for the center. After 3.e5 Bf5 — exactly like the Advance Caro-Kann. Or 3.exd5 cxd5 — Exchange, easy equality. White's f4 is premature and weakens the kingside.
d5.follow_up_idea: After 2...d5 3.e5 Bf5 — get the bishop out before playing e6. Same plan as the main Advance Caro. White's early f4 doesn't help their position.

strategic_ideas: White plays 2.Bc4 against your French Defense. Common at 200-500 ELO.
Your response: 2...d5! — challenge the center. After 3.exd5 exd5 4.Bb3 — the bishop retreats and you've equalized.
White's Bc4 doesn't target f7 effectively once you play ...e6 (unlike vs 1...e5).

d5.label: Correct — 2...d5!
d5.prose: Challenge the center immediately. After 3.exd5 exd5 4.Bb3 — the bishop retreats, you have a great center. Or 3.e5 c5 4.d3 Nc6 — French Advance structure where the Bc4 is misplaced.
d5.follow_up_idea: After 2...d5 3.exd5 exd5 — develop with Nf6, Bd6, 0-0. You have a perfectly good position. The Bc4 vs French is simply not dangerous.

strategic_ideas: White plays the Wayward Queen (2.Qh5) against your French. Harmless but alarming for beginners.
Easiest response: 2...Nf6! — attack the queen and develop. After 3.Qf3 (retreat) or 3.Qe2 d5 — you're fine.
Also good: 2...g6 chasing the queen. Either way, White has wasted a move.

Nf6.label: Best — 2...Nf6!
Nf6.prose: Attack the queen and develop simultaneously. After 3.Qe2 d5 4.exd5 Nxd5 — Black has recaptured and has excellent piece activity. Or 3.Qf3 d5 4.exd5 — Exchange French, easy equality.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 2...Nf6 3.e5? Nd5 — Black's knight is actively centralized. The queen on h5 is misplaced and returns quickly. White wasted two moves.

g6.label: Also Good — 2...g6
g6.prose: Chase the queen. After 3.Qf3 Nf6 4.d4 d5 — French-like position. White's queen has wasted two moves.
g6.follow_up_idea: After 2...g6 3.Qf3 d5 4.exd5 exd5 — solid French Exchange position. You're fine.

strategic_ideas: You're playing Caro-Kann or French so you won't reach this position as Black — but opponents often play 1.e4 e5 systems. Know them to recognize and understand the game.
Most common at 200-700 ELO: Italian Game (2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4), Scholar's Mate attempt (2.Bc4), King's Gambit (2.f4), Vienna (2.Nc3).
If you ever play 1...e5, you need to know: Scholar's Mate is the #1 trap at 200-400 ELO.

Nf3.label: Best Move — Italian/Ruy Lopez
Nf3.prose: The most principled move. After 2.Nf3 Nc6 3.Bc4 (Italian) or 3.Bb5 (Ruy Lopez) — main-line chess. Your Caro/French background means you likely won't play this, but good to recognize.
Nf3.follow_up_idea: If you ever play 1...e5, learn: 2.Nf3 Nc6 and then 3.Bc4 Bc5 (Italian/Giuoco Piano) is the most common at 200-700 ELO. Play natural developing moves.

Bc4.label: Scholar's Mate Setup — COMMON
Bc4.prose: Very common at 200-500 ELO! After 2.Bc4 Nc6 3.Qh5 (Scholar's Mate attempt) you must play 3...Nf6! — NOT 3...g6?? 4.Qxf7#. Also fine: 2...Nf6 or 2...Bc5.
Bc4.follow_up_idea: If you ever face 2.Bc4 after 1.e4 e5 — the critical line is 2...Nc6 3.Qh5 Nf6! (mandatory). Never play 3...g6?? which allows Qxf7 checkmate.

f4.label: King's Gambit — Common at Low ELO
f4.prose: White sacrifices a pawn for rapid development. After 2...exf4 (accepted) or 2...d5 (countered) — rich, tactical play. At 200-600 ELO this is common and dangerous if you don't know it.
f4.follow_up_idea: If you ever face the King's Gambit: 2...d5! (King's Gambit Counter) is the safest — 3.exd5 Nf6. Or accept with 2...exf4 and play ...g5 to hold the pawn.

Nc3.label: Vienna Game — Medium Common
Nc3.prose: After 2.Nc3 — can lead to Vienna Gambit (3.f4) or Vienna Game (3.Bc4). Common at 400-700 ELO. Play 2...Nc6 or 2...Nf6 for solid development.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 2.Nc3 Nf6 3.f4 — Vienna Gambit. Play 3...d5! (the best counter). Or 2.Nc3 Nc6 3.Bc4 — Italian/Vienna, play 3...Bc5 for solid equality.

strategic_ideas: 2.Bc4 — the Bishop's Opening. The MOST COMMON opening at 200-400 ELO. Often a Scholar's Mate setup.
Safe responses: 2...Nc6 (natural development), 2...Nf6 (counterattack), 2...Bc5 (mirror).
WARNING: If you play 2...Nc6 and White plays 3.Qh5 — DO NOT play 3...g6?? (allows Qxf7#). Play 3...Nf6! instead.

Nc6.label: Natural — 2...Nc6
Nc6.prose: Develops and defends. BUT if 3.Qh5 — play 3...Nf6! (NOT g6??). After 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.Ng5 d5 (Two Knights) — sharp play. Or 3.Nc3 Bc5 — solid.
Nc6.follow_up_idea: After 2...Nc6 3.Qh5 Nf6! 4.Qxf7+?? Kxf7 5.— White has no attack. Or 4.d3 Bc5 5.Ng5 0-0 — Black is safe. The Scholar's Mate fails against 3...Nf6!

Nf6.label: Counterattack — 2...Nf6
Nf6.prose: Attack e4 immediately. After 3.d3 Nc6 4.Nf3 Bc5 — solid Italian-like position. Or 3.Nc3 Nc6 4.Nf3 — Two Knights-like.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: 2...Nf6 avoids Scholar's Mate entirely. After 3.d3 Nc6 4.Nf3 Bc5 — completely solid development.

Bc5.label: Mirror — 2...Bc5
Bc5.prose: The Giuoco Piano mirror. After 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.Nc3 Nc6 — symmetrical, solid, normal development.
Bc5.follow_up_idea: After 2...Bc5 3.Nf3 Nc6 4.Nc3 — both sides develop naturally. Very solid for Black.

strategic_ideas: 3.Qh5 — the Scholar's Mate attempt. The most common trap at 200-500 ELO. Threatens 4.Qxf7#.
THE ONLY CORRECT RESPONSE: 3...Nf6! — attack the queen AND defend f7.
NEVER play 3...g6?? 4.Qxf7# (checkmate!). NEVER play 3...Qe7?? (blocks the bishop). Play 3...Nf6!

Nf6.label: ONLY CORRECT MOVE — 3...Nf6!
Nf6.prose: 3...Nf6 attacks the queen AND defends f7. After 4.Qxf7+?? Kxf7 5.— White has lost their queen for nothing. After 4.d3 Bc5 5.Ng5 0-0 6.Nxf7?? Rxf7 7.Bxf7+ Kxf7 — Black is material up. Scholar's Mate FAILS.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 3...Nf6 4.d3 Bc5 5.Nc3 — normal Italian-like game. White's queen has been wasted on h5. Black has easy development and a comfortable game.

strategic_ideas: The Italian Game — the most popular opening at 200-700 ELO. White targets f7 with the bishop.
Black's main responses: 3...Bc5 (Giuoco Piano — symmetric, solid), 3...Nf6 (Two Knights — active).
Note: you play Caro-Kann/French, so you won't face this as Black — but understanding it helps.

Bc5.label: Giuoco Piano (Most Common)
Bc5.prose: Both bishops face each other — the Giuoco Piano. White plays 4.c3 preparing d4 (main line) or 4.b4 (Evans Gambit — aggressive but unsound at top level). Very common at all club levels.
Bc5.follow_up_idea: After 3...Bc5 4.c3 Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ 7.Nc3 — modern Italian main line. Black has active piece play. Solid and well-tested.

Nf6.label: Two Knights — Active
Nf6.prose: Counterattacks e4. White often plays 4.Ng5 (attacking f7) — sharp! After 4...d5 5.exd5 Na5 — Black has active piece play. Or 4.d3 — quieter.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 3...Nf6 4.Ng5 d5 5.exd5 Na5 6.Bb5+ c6 7.dxc6 bxc6 8.Be2 h6 9.Nf3 e4 — Black has good compensation for the pawn.

strategic_ideas: The Giuoco Piano — both bishops are symmetrically placed. White's main plan is 4.c3 + d4.
Most common at 200-700 ELO: White plays c3 and then d4 to challenge the center.
Black's response: 4...Nf6 (develop) and if 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ — active counterplay.

c3.label: Main Line — 4.c3 + d4
c3.prose: White prepares the d4 pawn break. After 4...Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ 7.Nc3 — modern Italian. Or 5.d4 exd4 6.e5 (aggressive) Ne4 7.Bd5 — complex.
c3.follow_up_idea: After 4.c3 Nf6 5.d4 exd4 6.cxd4 Bb4+ 7.Nc3 Nxe4 — the game is sharp and roughly equal.

b4.label: Evans Gambit — Romantic but Risky
b4.prose: The Evans Gambit! Common at lower levels because it's fun. After 4...Bxb4 5.c3 Ba5 6.d4 — White gets a huge center for a pawn. Unsound at the top level but very dangerous in practice.
b4.follow_up_idea: Against the Evans Gambit: 4...Bxb4 (accept) 5.c3 Ba5 6.d4 exd4 7.0-0! — White has scary compensation. Or decline with 4...Bb6.

entries.variation_name: 1.e4 e5 2.f4 — Classic Gambit, Common at Low ELO
strategic_ideas: The King's Gambit! Very common at 200-600 ELO. White sacrifices a pawn for rapid attack.
Best response: 2...d5! (King's Gambit Counter) — simplest and most solid. Or 2...exf4 (accept it).
If you play Caro/French you won't face this — but useful to recognize if an opponent plays it.
Note: since you play 1...c6 or 1...e6, you won't see the King's Gambit in your games.

d5.label: Safest Counter — 2...d5
d5.prose: The King's Gambit Counter! After 3.exd5 Nf6 — Black attacks the d5 pawn and develops. Or 3.fxe5 dxe4 4.Nc3 — complex. This is the most solid approach.
d5.follow_up_idea: After 2...d5 3.exd5 Nf6 4.Nc3 Nxd5 — Black has excellent piece activity and has defused the gambit. Solid equality.

exf4.label: Accept — 2...exf4
exf4.prose: Accept the gambit! After 3.Nf3 d6 4.d4 g5 (holding the pawn) — sharp play. Or 3.Nf3 Nf6 4.Bc4 — active piece play. White has a center, Black has an extra pawn.
exf4.follow_up_idea: If you accept the King's Gambit: don't try to hold the f4 pawn at all costs. Play ...d6, ...Nf6, and develop. The game will be sharp.

Bc5.label: Classical Decline — 2...Bc5
Bc5.prose: Decline the gambit and develop. After 3.Nf3 d6 4.Nc3 Nf6 — solid development. White's f4 push weakens their kingside.
Bc5.follow_up_idea: After 2...Bc5 3.Nf3 Nc6 — solid development. The King's Gambit Declined gives Black a fine game.

strategic_ideas: The Vienna Game — 2.Nc3 develops the knight and prepares f4 or Bc4. Very common at 400-700 ELO.
Black's main response: 2...Nf6 (development) or 2...Nc6 (symmetry).
Warning: 3.f4 Vienna Gambit is very tricky — counter with 3...d5!

Nf6.label: Active — 2...Nf6
Nf6.prose: Attack e4 immediately. If 3.f4 (Vienna Gambit) — play 3...d5! 4.fxe5 Nxe4 — excellent counterplay. If 3.Bc4 Nxe4 4.Qh5 Nd6 — tricky but playable.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 2...Nf6 3.f4 d5! 4.fxe5 Nxe4 5.Nxe4 dxe4 — Black has a powerful passed pawn and active pieces. The Vienna Gambit is refuted by 3...d5!

Nc6.label: Symmetric — 2...Nc6
Nc6.prose: Natural development. After 3.f4 (Vienna Gambit) — play 3...Nf6! or 3...d5! to counter. If 3.Bc4 — Italian/Vienna Four Knights.
Nc6.follow_up_idea: Against Vienna: the key is 3.f4 d5! — always counter in the center when White tries a flank attack.

entries.variation_name: 1.e4 d5 — Very Common at Low ELO
strategic_ideas: The Scandinavian Defense — Black immediately challenges e4. Very popular at 200-600 ELO.
White takes: 2.exd5 — always take. After 2...Qxd5 3.Nc3 — the queen must move again.
You don't play this — but you'll see opponents play it constantly at 200-700 ELO.

exd5.label: Correct — 2.exd5
exd5.prose: Always take. After 2...Qxd5 3.Nc3 — the queen must move again (3...Qa5 most popular, 3...Qd6 or 3...Qe6 also seen). White has a development lead.
exd5.follow_up_idea: After 2.exd5 Qxd5 3.Nc3 Qa5 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 — White has a natural, solid center and development lead. The Scandinavian is Black's choice but White is slightly better.

strategic_ideas: Black recaptures with the queen. White plays 3.Nc3 to attack it.
The queen must move: 3...Qa5 (main), 3...Qd6 (passive), 3...Qe5+ (awkward) or 3...Qe6 (unusual).
White gains a development lead by attacking the queen repeatedly.

Nc3.label: Attack the Queen — 3.Nc3
Nc3.prose: Develop and attack. After 3...Qa5 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 — you have a comfortable development lead. The queen on a5 is slightly awkward.
Nc3.follow_up_idea: After 3.Nc3 Qa5 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 Bf5 6.Bc4 e6 7.Bd2 — solid development. White is slightly better due to the development lead.

strategic_ideas: White attacks the queen on d5. Black plays 3...Qa5 (main line) or 3...Qd6.
3...Qa5 is best — keeps the queen active and prepares ...c6 for a solid structure.
After 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 — White has easy development. The Scandinavian is solid but slightly passive.

Qa5.label: Main Line — 3...Qa5
Qa5.prose: The queen goes to a5 — active position, can later go to b6 or c7. After 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 Bf5 or 5...c6 — the Scandinavian main line.
Qa5.follow_up_idea: After 3...Qa5 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 Bg4 — Black develops the bishop before playing ...e6. White maintains a slight but comfortable advantage.

Qd6.label: Kloosterboer — 3...Qd6
Qd6.prose: Less common. The queen on d6 is passive but safe. After 4.d4 Nf6 5.Nf3 — White develops normally.
Qd6.follow_up_idea: 3...Qa5 is stronger. The queen on d6 blocks the d-file and has fewer active prospects.

strategic_ideas: The Ruy Lopez — the most sophisticated opening at club level. Common at 500-700+ ELO.
The Bb5 bishop indirectly attacks e5 by pinning the Nc6. White plans Bxc6 + Nxe5.
You won't face this with Caro/French — but knowing it helps recognize opponent setups.

a6.label: Morphy Defense — Most Common
a6.prose: 3...a6 forces the bishop to declare: 4.Ba4 (main), 4.Bxc6 (Exchange Ruy). After 4.Ba4 Nf6 5.0-0 — the Ruy Lopez main line. Very theoretical.
a6.follow_up_idea: The Ruy Lopez main line after 3...a6 4.Ba4 Nf6 5.0-0 Be7 6.Re1 b5 7.Bb3 is one of chess's most analyzed positions.

Nf6.label: Berlin Defense — Popular at All Levels
Nf6.prose: 3...Nf6 is the 'Berlin Wall' — very solid, leads to an endgame where Black's king walks to the center. Made famous by Kramnik's World Championship victory. Very common at 400-700 ELO because it's solid.
Nf6.follow_up_idea: After 3...Nf6 4.0-0 Nxe4 5.d4 Nd6 6.Bxc6 dxc6 7.dxe5 Nf5 — the Berlin Endgame. Black is solid but slightly passive.

strategic_ideas: White plays 2.f4 against your French — unusual and not great for White.
Play 2...d5! — fight for the center. After 3.e5 c5 4.Nf3 Nc6 — French Advance structure where White's f4 is a weakness.
You have a comfortable game — White's kingside is weakened without compensation.

d5.label: Correct — 2...d5
d5.prose: Challenge the center. After 3.e5 c5 4.Nf3 Nc6 — French Advance setup where your pieces are active. White's f4 pawn weakens f2-f3 area without attacking anything useful.
d5.follow_up_idea: After 2...d5 3.e5 c5 4.Nf3 Nc6 5.c3 Qb6 — French Advance plans apply. White's f4 is a weakness, not a strength, in this structure.

strategic_ideas: White plays 2.d3 — the King's Indian Attack setup. Quiet and solid but slow.
Your plan is the same: 2...d5 — fight for the center. After 3.Nd2 e5 4.Ngf3 Nd7 — solid French-like structure.
The KIA is not dangerous if you fight for the center immediately.

d5.label: Correct — 2...d5
d5.prose: Fight for the center immediately. After 3.Nd2 e5 4.Ngf3 Nd7 5.g3 Ngf6 6.Bg2 Bd6 — solid French-like position. White's KIA setup is passive against active Black play.
d5.follow_up_idea: In KIA vs Caro: always fight for ...e5 + ...d5 pawn center. After 3.Nd2 e5 you have two center pawns vs White's one. Develop normally and castle.
